sentences.text: In that little store in our apartment house, we noticed coming and going of certain elements who we have become to know.
And we knew they were right-wingers.
It turned out that that was one of the so-called cells, one little-- I don't want is the American word for that.
One of the nucleus-- was a Nazi nucleus there.
And we mentioned it.
He was a neighbor.
And my dad mentioned it to him.
We didn't want to go to the police and denounce him because they would-- at that time, Nazis had already been declared illegal in Austria, the same as the Schutzbund also was illegal.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we mentioned it to him and said, be careful.
We noticed it.
Other people will, too.
And not all were friendly at that time to the Nazis.
We weren't either, but we told the neighbor.
And he was very happy about it and promised that, that they'll be careful.
And if there should ever be a time, he will not forget us.
He didn't.
And otherwise, we realized, all of a sudden, white knee stockings appeared all over the street.
That was the illegal uniform of the Nazis.
And Sundays, in the Vienna woods, small groups of those got together.
We knew they were training and marching, and so on.
But the police was heavily infested with them, too.
So really nothing much happened.
Occasionally, a fight broke out.
Then it got in the newspaper.
We knew they existed.
We knew they were big in Germany.
And we knew that in Germany, the persecution of the Jews had started.
But we were absolutely convinced it couldn't happen in Austria.
Austria was too liberal minded, too progressive, too much left oriented.
It-- there were 180,000 Jews in Vienna alone, 10% of the population.
It couldn't happen.
Well, we were wrong.
And that was the Nazis immediately-- as soon as Hitler had taken over in '38 in an amazing, short time of a few days, he had really taken over Vienna and Austria.
We knew that, in the provinces, they were-- especially in the northern part in Tyrol, in upper Austria, in Vorarlberg, which is-- they were influenced.
They had contact with Germany.
Hitler supported the underground in Vienna actively with money and political support.

And many of the Christian socialists, the Vaterlandische Front-- which was the official organization of Schuschnigg and his predecessor, Dollfuss-- they were not at all very much opposed to the principles of the German Nazis.
Of course, after Dollfuss got killed by the Nazis and the party became legal, it was difficult.
But the Nazis had grown strong.
And since the left wing-- the left-wing party was also suppressed, those who could afford it religion-wise joined them just out of pure opposition to the government.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yes.
I had one of my friends who owned a motorcycle.
And he once invited me if I want to join him on [NON-ENGLISH] on the-- [LAUGHS] on the back seat of his motorcycle for a two- or three-day trip to Budapest.
Of course, I went along.
And in Budapest, sightseeing, we went up to the, the Fisher Bastion and all over Budapest.
And at one spot, I noticed the swastika.
And I went there.
And that was a fairly large poster.
I did-- I couldn't read it because it said, 'Eleg volt!' It didn't mean anything to me.
But I realized that Hungary already had been invaded, influenced, or infected by the bug.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was my only impression.
Otherwise, I liked Budapest very much.
It's a nice city, second to Vienna.
And I wrote at that time it's certainly a very non-Aryan people, the Hungarians.
But there, the Nazis were already making their inroad.
In Vienna, all over the streets were illegal, tolerated swastikas.
People were marching.
They had official gatherings, assemblies in the various halls in Vienna.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It just-- they were not suppressed anymore.
And we knew that.
Immediately after the Anschluss, they came out.
And within-- it was on a Friday afternoon.
We were in our store.
And we-- that Friday afternoon, we were supposed to prepare for the plebiscite.
Sunday was supposed to be the plebiscite for Anschluss to Germany or for independence of Austria.
That was Schuschnigg's last act before he went to Berchtesgaden and was convinced.
But we had-- as store owners, we had been given official posters to put in our windows.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Vote for independence.
Until-- and from the, the unions, from the outlying districts, they marched into the street-- or demonstrating for independence.
Then came the radio announcement that Schuschnigg had capitulated, had ordered the Austrian Army not to resist the German invasion, to avoid bloodshed.

And the same masses of people who had marched into the inner city in the afternoon, three hours laters, marched back to their outlying district already with swastika armbands and yelling the Sieg Heil, and, 'We love our Fuhrer,' and 'Down with the Jews,' 'Kill the Jews.' And we quickly took those posters out of our windows.
And we knew it had-- hell had started.
And next morning when we went down, opened our store, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the police already had swastika armbands.
And we knew that we as Jews were in mortal danger.
And we were.
Within three days-- Monday morning-- the Nazis came to our apartment, pulled my parents out of their beds.
They didn't give mother even a chance to put on a robe.
In her nightgown, they pulled her out of bed.
And my dad, who was sedated because of war injuries, he didn't even know for 10 minutes what happened.
I had opened the door because I was sleeping in, in the front door.
And there came two SS and two SA men.
First thing which entered the door when I got it finally open was a handgun, followed by a black-- by a black sleeve.

And then the, the four guys came in, ransacked the entire apartment, went in, threw everything out of the linen closets and the closets out on the floor looking for whatever they could use-- every document, money.
Whatever they found, they confiscated.
And then they marched my dad and myself down across the street to our store and did the same there, ransacked the store.
But the sewing operators-- the sewing machine operators were already waiting in front of the store because we started our store at 7:00.
These people came at 6:00 to our apartment Monday morning.
And whatever money they had found upstairs in our apartment-- there was quite a sum because we had prepared bills to be paid Monday morning.
They took that.
They left us the bills, but they took the money.
And they handed a good part of it out to the sewing machine operators, telling them, oh, that Jews was exploiting you anyway.
There you have victory bonuses.
And that was our very first experience in our own family.
And that happened Monday after they had taken over on Friday.
I was in our store [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] getting ready for the regular Saturday-- or Friday night and Saturday business when we heard that on the-- on the radio.
And immediately, all over the neighborhood, the, the shutters came down.
The stores were closed.
And we proceeded in all speed to remove the, the posters from our windows, which had said, vote against the Anschluss.
That was Friday night.
At night, we then sat in a neighbor's apartment, listening to-- he had a large radio.
And he got some foreign stations.
They were Czech citizens, so they were not immediately endangered.
And we listened to them.

It was constantly funeral music and a repeat of Schuschnigg's address, and the indication that the German are on the march, and had invaded already the northern provinces of Austria-- were on their march to Vienna.
No.
We were-- we were-- in that way, we were lucky.
First of all, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were not that organized immediately that they could do that.
They just cleaned us out as far as money and documents.
My passport was confiscated.
My parents-- we had-- we had a big, iron safe where we kept nothing, just our documents.
We had to open that.
They were looking for money.
There was no money in there, too bad.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they just-- they just made it difficult.
And, of course, immediately our windows-- our display windows on the stores were marked 'Jew' and a swastika, which prevented non-Jews and the neighborhood people from even coming into our store.
Off-- our business was finished.
We just stopped for a moment because there was a lot of noise.
And hopefully, that noise will be gone, or it's diminished, and that now we're starting again.
All right, we [LAUGHS] were talking about the Anschluss.
And we were talking about your-- I think, when we stopped, you were telling us about what happened with-- With our store.
Well, after they-- after they had ransacked our apartment, they marched my dad and myself over into the store, gave that money to the sewing operators, and left with my father under arrest.
While they were in the store, a telephone call came.
But they didn't let me go to the telephone.
And I knew exactly what that telephone call meant.
My girlfriend was calling at that time every day.
And that was tradition.

And when another voice picked up, she put two and two together and hung up and notified my brother, who was working in the neighborhood, in our neighborhood, for an English-owned company, who, at that time, was not in immediate danger.
She called him.
He had a brother-in-law from his side who somehow had a connection to the Nazis.
And he pulled strings.
And at night at around 6 o'clock, my dad came home.
We hadn't known where they took him and what happened to him.
But he came home unharmed-- physically unharmed, but shattered morally and emotionally for three weeks.
For three weeks, he sat in the dark storage room in our-- in our place.
And he didn't want to talk to anyone.
He's-- was fighting it.
They actually mistreated him.
If you want to know what they did to him, he had to take a necktie off.
Jews don't have to wear neckties.
He had to stand in the corner, face the wall.
Jews don't have to see what's going on in the room, and things like that.
They didn't beat him.
They didn't touch him.
But morally, they diminished him.
And he was a very respectable man who had done his duty, brought up the family, served in the army.
And all of a sudden, here was nothing.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later on, Tuesday or Wednesday, that neighbor who we had warned years before, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he came to our rescue in the way that he got a certificate, which we posted on our door, signed by the local Nazi authorities, testifying that Mr.
Sigfried Schneider, the owner of the store, has served in the Austrian Army with distinction, medals, and so on and so forth.
And that alone, signed by the Gauleiter, you know, the big shot, that alone helped to prevent further incursion into our store by the SA or SS.
But customers still, still didn't come to us.
We had our store in that place for 29 years.
We were well-known in the neighborhood.
There were a lot of people who have done [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] business with us on a month-to-month payment business.
And all of a sudden, it became known that Hitler had declared that debts to Jews can be forgotten.
We lost a lot of money because those people can't talk.
Fine.
What we bought last Christmas doesn't have to be paid now-- or what we have bought during the time.
There were hardly anyone who came and paid.
One of our sewing-machine operators came back to us and said-- gave us the money back she had gotten from the victory bonus.
She gave it back to us.
And who was that girl?
A Jewish-- [LAUGHS] a Jewish girl who had been working for us, the only one.
At the same time, we were dispossessed.
And we got what they called a commisar.
It was somebody in charge.
And the commisar, the pick for the sewing room, was the senior-- the oldest of the sewing-machine operators, a woman named Trsek, a real, Aryan name, T-R-S-E-K.
And she was supposed to be supervising the stores from then on.
She was a sewing-machine operator.
But she didn't know anything else.
And she couldn't cut it.
And they depended on-- still on me.
I was a cutter in the store.
So anyway-- and middle of the next week, then we, we got regular commisar.
He came in the morning, made sure everything is all right, the people are there, and checked what was in the cash register, and left us to our own devices.
And at night, he came, checked the cash register and took a certain amount of money out, left us enough to live, and disappeared again.
He stayed there long after I was already out of Austria.
But he was-- he was a decent-- a decent citizen.
Hitler came in on the Westbahn, the west-- the main-- the main line and about 15 minutes walk from-- not even from our store.
We were in a side street, but only two houses below the main-- the main drag.
There was the main route to the center, leading directly into the center of the city.
And of course, I went up, and I stood at the corner when, when those troops marched in.
And there I saw a convertible surrounded by motorcycles and preceded by all kind of cars.
And there was Friend Adolf standing in the-- in the convertible with his upraised hand coming into the city.
I have seen him alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was all I-- all I knew, the only impression.
I said, that's Hitler.
We knew who he was.
We knew what he did in Germany.
We knew what he had done to us by then.
And so I happened to see there.
Of course, I didn't say anything, couldn't do anything.
I didn't welcome him.
But the reception was phenomenal.
Especially the women went absolutely crazy.
There are pictures around, also in the book I have here, showing how wildly they welcomed it.
The government-- our own government in Austria were so hated and despised by the overall population, with minor exceptions, that anything against them was welcomed.
And, of course, we paid the price.
No, was coming in on the main-- on the Mariahilfer Strasse, which led down to the ring.
They were hiding.
Yeah.
No, no.
There were masses of people lining the street like here on the-- at the Rose Parade, maybe not quite as many, but solidly packed.
And people were waving Nazi flocks-- flags.
And there were plenty in supply.
And once he was gone, they dispersed.
Very much isolated.
I knew from experience that I was rarely recognized as a Jew visually.
I could go anywhere in the city.
I knew I was not allowed to wear the swastika, which most of the other people wore.
I was not allowed-- I would not go out in white stockings anymore.
But otherwise, facial expression, behavior, speech, was not Jewish.
Some people who live in the Second District in Leopoldstadt, many of them had still a heavy accent from Poland or Yiddish.
But I could pass anywhere as a non-Jew.
As a matter of fact, later on, when I went to, to Berlin, I had a very difficult time, but still, that helped.
I was not recognizable as a Jew.
Well, my girlfriend was the one who had called to talk to me.
I had met her on a blind date in September, 1937.
And at the beginning, she didn't want to meet me again.
And I found out later on she had been engaged to a Catholic, had broken that engagement because of the religion difference.
And when we met, she did not know that I was Jewish.
And she didn't want to make the same mistake twice.
So it took some persuasion on my part to get her to meet me again.
And to that meeting, I brought along my [GERMAN], my birth certificate.
The Christians had a certificate of baptism, which was issued by their local parish church.
The Jews got a birth certificate from the Jewish community.
So I brought that along.
Proof-- here it is.
And we fell in love in September.
October, November, we had a wonderful time.
We danced every single occasion we had.
We went to all the balls.
And--
Her name was Adele [?
Spurling. ?] She became my wife in 1939 in Brooklyn.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was the one who called that morning and contacted my brother.
Two days later, she lost her job, too.
And she moved in with us.
Yes.
I can tell you a story about that.
Must have been about a week later.
I don't know the exact date.
We got a telephone call at home, quote, 'It would be advisable for the young Herr Snyder,' myself, 'not to be home today,' hung up.
I took the hint, got dressed, what little money I had, not much, and left home.
And I spent the day riding the trolley and the subway all over Vienna, just not to be home.

When I got home at night, I found out that a couple of hours later, mid-morning, they had sealed the street on both ends and had brought in some SA or maybe SS troopers with some army-- with some trucks, had pulled Jews out of the apartments and the stores, and forced them to crawl underneath those vehicles and scrub them with pails and brushes and water and scrub the street.
And one incident my parents told me--
